mappings.rationaleAI: Freud psychoanalytic theory of personality — id, ego, superego — directly constitutes A.2; his clinical accounts of defense mechanisms cover C.3; and his theories of neurosis are foundational to D.1 and D.3.

Directly covers all definitions of personality (A), from physiological to psychoanalytic (A.2) to trait and factor-analytic frameworks including the five-factor model (A.4), and explicitly includes measurement (B, B.1, B.2).

As the parent discipline, psychology encompasses all theoretical approaches to personality (A), psychological assessment methods (B), adjustment and functioning (C), and mental disorder classification (D.1-D.3).

Covers emotion dynamics and regulation as strains on personality functioning (C.2) and responses to stress through emotional processing (C.3); affective disorders listed under D.1 are categorized by emotional dysregulation.
Covers the mind conscious/unconscious processes foundational to personality definitions (A), the development of self-awareness (C.1), and mental disorder as disturbance of mind (D).
Meditation as a technique for emotional regulation and stress management constitutes a coping and adjustment dynamism (C.3).
Covers the full range of behavioral, cognitive, and emotional patterns shaping personality (A), and specifically addresses antisocial behavior, conflict, stress as strains on functioning (C.2, C.3).

Mental health dimensions — stress, psychological well-being — connect to strains on personality functioning (C.2); health determinants and disparities relate to epidemiology of mental disorders (D.2).
Directly covers the distinction between mental health and mental illness, risk factors across biological and socioeconomic dimensions (D.3), treatment approaches, and disparities.
Sexual orientation connects to social analytic personality theories regarding identity (A.3), and the DSM-era history of classification is relevant to D.1.
Sadness as a basic emotion functions as a psychological strain (C.2); its severe form maps to affective disorders under D.1.
Covers awareness and self-awareness central to the development of self (C.1), and phenomenological accounts of consciousness underlie personality theories (A).
Fear is a primary psychological strain (C.2); phobias map to anxiety disorders under D.1; and defensive responses exemplify adjustment dynamisms (C.3).
Happiness and well-being constitute the positive pole of personality functioning and adjustment (C); connects to self-fulfillment (C.1) and strains on functioning (C.2).
Autism spectrum condition falls under neurodevelopmental disorders (D.1); its rising prevalence maps to D.2; and its genetic heritability addresses causation (D.3).
Addiction as a neuropsychological disorder maps to D.1; its multifactorial etiology addresses D.3; and substance misuse constitutes a strain on personality functioning (C.2).
Directly covers all three sub-paths under D: major categories including anxiety, mood, psychotic, and personality disorders (D.1); epidemiology (D.2); and multifactorial causation (D.3).
Suicide is linked to mental illness (D.1) with risk factors spanning psychosocial stressors (D.3); represents an extreme response to strains on personality functioning (C.2).
Humour serves stress relief and psychological adjustment functions (C.3); relief theory specifically theorizes it as a mechanism for coping with psychological tension.
Parenting styles are social-environmental factors shaping personality development (A.3) and self-awareness (C.1); parental psychopathology influences causation of mental disorders (D.3).
Adolescence is the key period of identity formation (C.1); peer pressure and role confusion constitute psychological strains (C.2); and onset of many mental disorders occurs in this period (D.1).
Gender identity and roles are core to social analytic personality theories (A.3) and eclectic role theories (A.4); gender identity formation is central to development of self (C.1).
Descartes mind-body dualism is foundational to physiological and philosophical theories of personality (A.1).
Anthropology cross-cultural study of personality informs social analytic personality theories (A.3) and eclectic role theories (A.4).
